hero_image.alt: Portrait of W. E. B. Du Bois, 1907
hero_image.caption: W. E. B. Du Bois in 1907, four years after The Souls of Black Folk appeared.
hero_image.license: Public domain; NPG digital image released CC0

inline_images.alt: Four African American women students seated on the steps of a building at Atlanta University, Georgia, 1899 or 1900
inline_images.caption: Four students on the steps at Atlanta University around 1900, from W. E. B. Du Bois's own album of African American life, embodying the college-trained leadership he champions in this chapter.
inline_images.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (Daniel Murray Collection, assembled by W. E. B. Du Bois for the 1900 Paris Exposition)
inline_images.license: Public domain (no known restrictions on publication)

related_chapters.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
related_chapters.part_title: A Family of Twelve in Warren County
related_chapters.chapter_title: This One Will Go to College
related_chapters.topic: A Mother's Hope and a We
related_chapters.why: Du Bois insists on opening the college door to the striving few; Terry recounts a mother's hope that singled out one child to go to college.

related_chapters.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
related_chapters.why: The essay frames the Black college as the seedbed of trained leaders; Thelwell recounts arriving at Howard University in 1959.

related_chapters.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
related_chapters.why: Du Bois argues education must develop the whole person, not just a workman; Sellers reflects on the movement itself as a university that trained him.

related_chapters.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
related_chapters.chapter_title: It Does Not Have to Be This Way
related_chapters.why: Du Bois calls for a Talented Tenth of college-trained Black thinkers; Hrabowski recounts realizing the Meyerhoff Scholars program to produce Black PhDs in science.

related_chapters.subject: Glenda Funchess
related_chapters.why: The essay weighs the public schools as the chief means of training citizens; Funchess recounts the stair-step plan by which the schools began to desegregate.

related_chapters.subject: Timothy Jenkins
related_chapters.why: Du Bois ties higher training to leaders who think rather than untrained agitators; Jenkins describes Howard students taking on a more radical dimension as the sit-ins began.

top_voice.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
top_voice.reason: Hrabowski's lifework building a pipeline of college-trained Black scientists is the most direct modern realization of the essay's argument for higher education and a Talented Tenth.

closing_image.alt: Freeman A. Hrabowski III at the 2012 Time 100 gala
closing_image.caption: Freeman A. Hrabowski III, the corpus voice this essay connects to most. Hrabowski's lifework building a pipeline of college-trained Black scientists is the most direct modern realization of the essay's argument for higher education and a Talented Tenth.

connections.summary: Across the Civil Rights History Project, the oral histories below take up the questions Of the Training of Black Men raises. Our embedding model places it nearest the testimony of John Carlos, Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson, and Amos C. Brown.
